nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, generally found in small to large groups over live coral patches in silty lagoon and inshore reef areas, typically at depths of roughly 2-14 m.

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of frozen mysis and brine shrimp, dried seaweed or nori, herbivore flakes or pellets, and quality marine flake foods. Multiple small feedings per day tend to be advised.

avoidWith: other Pomacentrus damsels in small tanks
very docile slow movers like firefish in confined systems
similarly sized damsels of the same genus

summary: A hardy Western Pacific damselfish with a silvery to pale-pink body and a developing blue-purple wash around the eyes. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, though tends toward conspecific territoriality as it matures.
careNotes: The Pink Smith Damselfish is commonly considered one of the more peaceable Pomacentrus damsels and is widely sold as a hardy, beginner-friendly reef fish. It is generally regarded as fully reef-safe with corals and ornamental invertebrates. Like other Pomacentrus, it tends to become more territorial toward similar-genus or similarly sized damsels as it matures, so a longer footprint with several defensible territories in the rockwork is generally advised. Although retailers commonly cite a 30-US-gallon (~114 L) minimum, biasing toward ~120 L or larger with open swimming space tends to reduce aggression and is more in line with reports that the species does best in small groups (sometimes recommended as odd-numbered groups of nine or more in larger systems). Temperatures reported across hobby sources cluster around 22-26°C, while FishBase's wild data gives 25-29.3°C; the 24-28°C range used here reflects an average across captive and wild references. Captive-bred specimens (e.g., ORA) are available and tend to acclimate more readily than wild-collected fish.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning. Males prepare and guard demersal eggs that adhere to a chosen substrate. Captive breeding has been reported (including commercial captive-bred lines), but rearing the pelagic larvae through their 1-2 month planktonic phase is generally considered difficult.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Husbandry baseline: 30 US gal minimum, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, semi-aggressive, reef compatible, omnivore, Easy care level, max 2.75 in. Collection root (https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/damselfish) does not list the species directly; product page URL resolved via search.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, tank size, temperament, diet, care level, and native range (Indo-Pacific, Solomon).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Pomacentridae, Ovalentaria/misc), authority Fowler & Bean 1928, max 7.0 cm TL, depth 2-14 m, wild temperature 25-29.3°C (mean 28.6), Western Central Pacific (Philippines, PNG, Solomons, New Hebrides), trophic level 2.7, oviparous with male egg care. Used to widen the captive-temperature range toward wild values.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-check: 2.75 in max, 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.3, SG 1.020-1.025, 30 gal min, reef-safe, lifespan ~5 yr, recommends odd groups of 9+. Slightly tighter pH band than LiveAquaria; values averaged.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn on a cleaned substrate; males guard and fan demersal eggs. Larvae are pelagic for 1-2 months and historically considered difficult to rear, though commercial captive-bred lines (e.g., ORA) have been produced.
